fragments.content: Precision oncology tailors cancer treatment based on individual tumor molecular characteristics rather than tissue of origin. Genomic profiling approaches: targeted gene panels (50-500+ genes, most common clinical use); whole exome sequencing (all coding regions, ~20,000 genes); whole genome sequencing (entire genome including non-coding); RNA sequencing (gene expression, fusions). Types of alterations detected: single nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions/deletions (indels), copy number alterations (amplifications, deletions), gene fusions/rearrangements, tumor mutational burden (TMB), microsatellite instability (MSI). Actionable alterations have matched therapies with clinical evidence. Tumor-agnostic approvals: pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR tumors (2017) and TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb, 2019); larotrectinib/entrectinib for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (2018/2019); dabrafenib + trametinib for BRAF V600E solid tumors (2022). Challenges: variant interpretation (pathogenic vs VUS), tumor heterogeneity, clonal evolution, access/equity.

Treatment selection based on genomic findings. Evidence levels for actionability (ESMO ESCAT scale): Tier I - clinically actionable (FDA-approved for indication or strong evidence from prospective trials); Tier II - potentially actionable (early phase trials, retrospective data, biologic rationale); Tier III - preclinical evidence only. Basket trials enroll patients by molecular alteration regardless of tumor type: NCI-MATCH, ASCO TAPUR, MyPathway demonstrated feasibility but variable response rates by tumor type. Examples of matched therapies: EGFR mutations - osimertinib (lung, also investigational in other EGFR-mutant tumors); HER2 amplification/mutation - trastuzumab deruxtecan (breast, gastric, now pan-tumor approval 2024); BRAF V600E - dabrafenib + trametinib; NTRK fusions - larotrectinib/entrectinib; RET fusions - selpercatinib; MET exon 14 skipping - capmatinib/tepotinib. Combination strategies address resistance mechanisms. Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) enables monitoring of response and emerging resistance without repeat tissue biopsy. Molecular tumor boards review complex cases.

When and how to order genomic testing. Testing indications: metastatic/unresectable disease (NCCN recommends for most solid tumors); recurrent disease; rare cancers with limited standard options; consideration for tumor-agnostic therapies; clinical trial eligibility. Sample requirements: adequate tumor cellularity (>20%); FFPE tissue preferred; fresh frozen for research; liquid biopsy if tissue unavailable or inadequate. Turnaround time: 2-4 weeks for most commercial panels. Interpretation challenges: distinguishing driver from passenger mutations; clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) causing false positives in liquid biopsy; variants of uncertain significance (VUS) should not guide treatment. Incidental germline findings: 10-15% of somatic tests reveal potential germline mutations (BRCA1/2, Lynch syndrome genes, TP53, etc.); genetic counseling referral required for confirmed/suspected germline findings. Re-testing at progression identifies resistance mechanisms (EGFR C797S, ALK G1202R, KRAS G12C secondary mutations). Cost and reimbursement vary by payer and indication.
